answer:
'Ofermod'—often glossed as excessive or overweening pride—is a key vice in Old English Christian poetry, portrayed as a destructive swelling of the spirit that leads to malice, envy, deceit, and eternal damnation. In the poem "Vainglory" (ch. 12 of *Old English Elegies*), it is the central theme, rendered in the original as *oferhygdo* ("in his oferhygdo presses on with power"), *oferhygda* ("full of oferhygda"), *oferhygd* ("through oferhygda raises himself up"; "oferhygd arose among angels"), and *ofermedum* ("in ofermedum... lives in vice").[^1][^2][^3] These map to the translation's "vainglory" and "overweening pride," where the proud man boasts above his betters, kindled by envy at feasts, his "unseemly spirit swells up within him," becoming "the fiend's own child" destined for hellish torments like the rebelling angels who sought to usurp God's throne.[^4]

The poem contrasts this with *eaðmod* (humility), whose humble man—loving even enemies—is "God's own child" and ascends to heavenly joy.[^4] Elsewhere in the corpus, pride (*pryde*) appears as hubris in Cynewulf's *Andreas* (ch. 1), where heathens mock the saint: "Where is your glory, that you in your pride [*pryde*] raised up so high?"—echoing the fall motif—but without *ofermod* explicitly.[^5] No other works use the precise term based on searches of translations and originals.[^6]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 12 "Vainglory" (original), [p-390a47](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/12-vainglory/original/#p-390a47).
[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 12 "Vainglory" (transliteration), [p-ecc17c](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/12-vainglory/transliteration/#p-ecc17c).
[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 12 "Vainglory" (original), [p-1b0fce](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/12-vainglory/original/#p-1b0fce).
[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 12 "Vainglory" (translation), [p-946b37](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/12-vainglory/translation/#p-946b37).
[^5]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, ch. 1 (translation), [p-02e4ce](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-02e4ce).
[^6]: Corpus search exhausted for "ofermod," "oferhygd," and related; nearest thematic matches are Cynewulf's *Elene* and *Juliana* (ch. 1 each, TF-IDF sim. to "Vainglory").
